RiskFactors.explanation_investors: This bridge has 3 distinct vulnerability classes creating a critical compounding risk. Bridges with this profile have historically suffered catastrophic losses — the attack surface is multiplicative, not additive. When signature verification weaknesses, cross-chain message validation gaps, and execution control vulnerabilities are all present in the same protocol, the protocol's security depends on every layer holding simultaneously. The 1.4x multiplier reflects the reality that a single breach in any layer can cascade across the others.
RiskFactors.explanation_developers: CRITICAL: 3 distinct vulnerability vectors compound catastrophically. Vectors: signature verification weaknesses, cross-chain message validation gaps, and execution control vulnerabilities. A comprehensive bridge security audit addressing all vectors simultaneously is required — partial remediation is insufficient when attack paths can chain across layers. Commission an independent security audit before deploying or continuing to operate.

RiskFactors.full_evidence: Signature verification bypass — critical bridge vulnerability
RiskFactors.explanation_investors: This factor shows that signature verification bypass — critical bridge vulnerability. Investors should treat the protocol as exploitable until the issue is publicly remediated.
RiskFactors.explanation_developers: Harden the contracts so that signature verification bypass — critical bridge vulnerability is no longer possible. Deploy targeted tests, guards, and emergency procedures before redeploying.

cir_explanation.title: Bridge Signature Verification Bypass
cir_explanation.summary: Ensure bridge handlers verify unique nonces and enforce signature thresholds before calling mint or withdraw functions.

guidance.investor: The bridge's signature verification — the process that confirms withdrawals are legitimate — can be bypassed. Bridges require multiple signers to approve transactions (like a safe needing multiple keys). When replay protection or threshold checks are missing, an attacker can reuse old approvals or forge new ones to drain funds. The Poly Network lost $611M through exactly this class of signature bypass. Do not hold significant value on bridges where this weakness exists.
guidance.developer: Hash payloads with domain-separated replay IDs, enforce minimum signature thresholds, and reject already-processed message hashes before executing withdrawals.

RiskFactors.full_evidence: Cross-chain transaction replay protection missing or insufficient (write-then-delete pattern allows replay)
RiskFactors.explanation_investors: This factor shows that cross-chain transaction replay protection missing or insufficient (write-then-delete pattern allows replay). Investors should treat the protocol as exploitable until the issue is publicly remediated.
RiskFactors.explanation_developers: Harden the contracts so that cross-chain transaction replay protection missing or insufficient (write-then-delete pattern allows replay) is no longer possible. Deploy targeted tests, guards, and emergency procedures before redeploying.

cir_explanation.title: Bridge replay protection gaps
cir_explanation.summary: Bridge handlers lack nonce or transaction-hash tracking, so an attacker can replay old payloads to drain custody.

guidance.investor: The bridge does not track which withdrawal requests have already been processed. This means a single valid withdrawal receipt could be submitted over and over to drain the bridge treasury — like photocopying a cheque and cashing it multiple times. Avoid bridges that lack message-tracking safeguards.
guidance.developer: Persist VAA identifiers and revert if `consumed[vaaHash]` is already set before processing payloads.

RiskFactors.description: Dangerous write-then-delete replay guard pattern detected
RiskFactors.full_evidence: Dangerous write-then-delete replay guard pattern detected
RiskFactors.explanation_investors: This factor shows that dangerous write-then-delete replay guard pattern detected. Investors should treat the protocol as exploitable until the issue is publicly remediated.
RiskFactors.explanation_developers: Harden the contracts so that dangerous write-then-delete replay guard pattern detected is no longer possible. Deploy targeted tests, guards, and emergency procedures before redeploying.

cir_explanation.title: Flawed replay protection mechanism
cir_explanation.summary: The consumed-message mapping uses a hash that does not include `block.chainid`, or nonces are sequential and predictable, or a migration function can reset the tracking state.

guidance.investor: Flawed replay protection is worse than missing protection because it creates false confidence. Verify that the bridge's replay guard has been specifically tested in its audit.
guidance.developer: Include chain ID, contract address, and a unique message identifier in the replay guard hash. Use permanent storage for consumed messages and never allow the tracking state to be reset or migrated without cryptographic continuity.

RiskFactors.description: External calls in bridge execution without target...
RiskFactors.full_evidence: External calls in bridge execution without target allowlist or selector gating
RiskFactors.explanation_investors: This factor shows that external calls in bridge execution without target allowlist or selector gating. Investors should treat the protocol as exploitable until the issue is publicly remediated.
RiskFactors.explanation_developers: Harden the contracts so that external calls in bridge execution without target allowlist or selector gating is no longer possible. Deploy targeted tests, guards, and emergency procedures before redeploying.

cir_explanation.title: Unbounded external call loop
cir_explanation.summary: Functions iterate over recipient arrays (e.g., `for (uint i; i < recipients.length; i++) recipients[i].call(...)`) where a malicious recipient can cause the entire batch to revert or consume all block gas.

guidance.investor: Unbounded external calls can freeze protocol operations if any recipient reverts or consumes excessive gas. This can block withdrawals, distributions, or liquidations.
guidance.developer: Replace `for` loops with push/pull patterns (allow users to claim instead of batch-sending). If looping is required, use `gasleft()` checks and `try/catch` around each external call.

RiskFactors.full_evidence: Missing reentrancy protection in vulnerable functions
RiskFactors.explanation_investors: Functions that handle fund transfers lack reentrancy protection, making them vulnerable to attack. Without proper guards, attackers can exploit the contract's external calls to drain funds repeatedly.
RiskFactors.explanation_developers: Add OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard modifier to all functions that make external calls or transfer funds. Use the nonReentrant modifier consistently across vulnerable functions.

cir_explanation.title: Reentrancy exposure
cir_explanation.summary: This is the classic Solidity reentrancy pattern: `call`/`delegatecall` running before `balance` updates. A variant uses ERC-721/ERC-1155/ERC-3525 `safeTransferFrom` callbacks to re-enter through a different function (the callback) that independently modifies state. Apply the CEI pattern or a reentrancy guard to ALL entry points, including callbacks.

guidance.investor: Reentrancy is one of the most dangerous smart contract flaws. It lets an attacker call the 'withdraw' function over and over before the contract updates the balance — draining funds in a loop. This is exactly how The DAO lost $60M in 2016, the first major DeFi exploit. If a protocol has this vulnerability, your deposited funds are at immediate risk.
guidance.developer: Use the checks-effects-interactions pattern and a guard (e.g., mutex or OpenZeppelin ReentrancyGuard). Replace `call.value` with helpers that revert on failure.

RiskFactors.full_evidence: Incomplete domain separation - only 33% of components present
RiskFactors.explanation_investors: This factor shows that incomplete domain separation - only 33% of components present. Investors should treat the protocol as exploitable until the issue is publicly remediated.
RiskFactors.explanation_developers: Harden the contracts so that incomplete domain separation - only 33% of components present is no longer possible. Deploy targeted tests, guards, and emergency procedures before redeploying.

cir_explanation.title: Missing or incomplete domain separation
cir_explanation.summary: Signed payloads omit `block.chainid` or `address(this)` from the EIP-712 domain separator, so a message signed for Ethereum mainnet can be replayed on Arbitrum or a fork.

guidance.investor: Missing domain separation means signatures are portable between chains and contracts. This is a critical bridge vulnerability that expands the attack surface to every deployment of the same code.
guidance.developer: Use EIP-712 structured signatures with `DOMAIN_SEPARATOR = keccak256(name, version, chainId, verifyingContract)`. Recompute on chain ID change (hard fork protection).

exploit_meta.name: Ronin Bridge
exploit_meta.description: Compromise of 5 out of 9 validator private keys

sections.title: Not a Bug — a Key Heist
sections.text: March 23, 2022: North Korea’s Lazarus Group executes the largest bridge exploit in crypto history — not by finding a smart contract bug, but by stealing validator keys. Ronin’s Ethereum bridge ran Proof-of-Authority consensus requiring 5 of 9 validator signatures to authorise withdrawals. Sky Mavis operated four validators; Axie DAO held one; four others were distributed to external parties. The attackers compromised all four Sky Mavis keys through social engineering — spear-phishing and malware targeting internal systems — then reached the fifth through a lingering operational shortcut.

sections.title: The Forgotten Allowlist
sections.text: In late 2021, Axie DAO had temporarily allowed Sky Mavis to sign transactions on its behalf to handle a period of high network load. The allowlist was never fully revoked. This meant that whoever controlled Sky Mavis’s infrastructure effectively controlled five of nine validators — the exact threshold needed to authorise bridge withdrawals. The attackers used these five compromised keys to sign two withdrawal transactions: 173,600 ETH and 25.5 million USDC, totalling approximately $625M. From the bridge contract’s perspective, these were perfectly valid — five legitimate signatures, threshold met.

sections.title: Six Days in the Dark
sections.text: The bridge had no real-time anomaly detection, no withdrawal rate limiting, and no alerts for large outflows. The exploit went undetected for six days. It was only discovered on March 29 when a user tried to withdraw 5,000 ETH and the transaction failed — the bridge was insolvent. Six days of silence on a $625M theft. The stolen funds were laundered through Tornado Cash, cross-chain bridges, and eventually centralised exchanges — a pattern consistent with prior Lazarus Group operations. Law enforcement and Chainalysis later recovered approximately $30M.

sections.title: The Single-Org Failure Domain
sections.text: The FBI and U.S. Treasury formally attributed the attack to North Korea’s Lazarus Group. Sky Mavis raised $150M (led by Binance) to reimburse users, expanded the validator set, raised the signature threshold, and removed the legacy allowlist. But the core lesson is architectural: a 5-of-9 multisig where one organisation controls four keys and has a backdoor path to a fifth is a single-organisation failure domain. Notably, our engine flagged vulnerabilities in this contract that the attackers never even needed to exploit — missing replay protection in the signature verification path, and structural weaknesses in the bridge’s message validation. Lazarus took a simpler door, but the code had others.

breakdown.vulnerability: Stolen validator keys via social engineering — 5-of-9 threshold breached
breakdown.steps: 1) Lazarus Group compromises four Sky Mavis validator keys via spear-phishing and internal system infiltration. 2) A residual allowlist from late 2021 lets Sky Mavis sign on behalf of Axie DAO — attackers now control 5 of 9 keys. 3) Sign two fraudulent withdrawals: 173,600 ETH and 25.5M USDC ($625M total). 4) Bridge contract validates 5 signatures, threshold met — funds released. 5) Exploit goes undetected for 6 days until a user’s 5,000 ETH withdrawal fails. 6) Funds laundered via Tornado Cash and cross-chain bridges; ~$30M later recovered by law enforcement.
steps_list: Lazarus Group compromises four Sky Mavis validator keys via spear-phishing and internal system infiltration
A residual allowlist from late 2021 lets Sky Mavis sign on behalf of Axie DAO — attackers now control 5 of 9 keys
Sign two fraudulent withdrawals: 173,600 ETH and 25.5M USDC ($625M total)
Bridge contract validates 5 signatures, threshold met — funds released
Exploit goes undetected for 6 days until a user’s 5,000 ETH withdrawal fails
Funds laundered via Tornado Cash and cross-chain bridges; ~$30M later recovered by law enforcement
